method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is an uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, located about 40 km south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] It is part of the central Line Islands and consists of the crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano.[2] The island’s center is a dried former lagoon in which deep guano deposits accumulated.[2] The emergent land area of Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge covers 1,273 acres, while submerged lands and waters within the three-mile limit add 36,214 acres, for a total refuge area of 37,487 acres.[3]

The island is low-lying and mostly sandy, with a maximum elevation of about 7 m.[2] Jarvis has a tropical desert climate marked by high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sunlight, scant rainfall of less than 1 m per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[2] Its vegetation is sparse and consists mainly of bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs, with no plants larger than shrubs.[2] These terrestrial habitats provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering areas for seabirds, shorebirds, and land hermit crabs.[2]

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] It has been described as having 14 species of breeding seabirds and as the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[3] The island alone has been estimated to support about one million sooty terns.[1] After eradication of invasive animals, bird populations on Jarvis and nearby U.S. equatorial islands increased, with shearwaters returning to Jarvis.[1]

The marine ecosystem around Jarvis is unusually productive because of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling associated with eastward-flowing equatorial currents.[2] Its shallow benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side of the island.[2] The fringing reef has been characterized as a healthy coral community, reflecting the island’s remoteness and limited human impact.[3] The refuge is managed for preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special emphasis on its large seabird nesting colonies.[3]

==History==
The first recorded European sighting of Jarvis Island was on August 21, 1821, by the British ship Eliza Francis, which was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, after whom the island was named.[2] American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano deposits there, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed the island in 1840.[2] In 1857, Arthur Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner Liholiho, took possession of Jarvis Island for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS St. Mary subsequently surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States.[4]

Guano mining took place during the late 19th century, but the island was abandoned by the United States in 1879 after substantial quantities had been removed.[1][4] The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis Island in 1889, but did not develop the mining operations, and a later 1906 lease to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London also produced no further development.[4] The United States reclaimed Jarvis Island in 1935, and President Franklin D. Roosevelt placed it under the control and jurisdiction of the Secretary of the Interior by Executive Order 7368 on May 13, 1936.[3][5] During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island in 1942, leading to the evacuation of its colonists.[1]

On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its surrounding territorial sea out to three nautical miles as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also made part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System, whose mission is to conserve, manage and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plants and their habitats for the benefit of present and future generations.[2] Its stated refuge purpose at transfer to the Service was the “preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with particular emphasis on large seabird nesting colonies.[5]

Modern U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service materials list the refuge as encompassing 429,853 acres, of which 1,273 acres are terrestrial and 428,580 acres are submerged lands and waters.[2] The World Database on Protected Areas reports the protected area as 1,744.87 square kilometres and classifies it as a marine and coastal protected area.[1] Earlier refuge planning documents described the original boundary as including the island and its territorial sea outward to the three-mile limit, for a total of 37,487 acres.[5]

Management planning for the refuge has included a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service comprehensive conservation plan completed in 2008.[4] The refuge’s planning documents evaluate management alternatives and effects for actions such as field-camp configurations and conservation activities.[5] Public access and non-management uses are controlled through compatibility determinations; in one draft determination, the Service found that regulated amateur radio operation would not detract from the refuge’s core conservation purposes.[2]

Scientific and management visits are infrequent because of the island’s remoteness; a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service biology team visit in August 2024 was described as the agency’s first trip to Jarvis in more than six years.[3] That visit included rapid field assessments by personnel from the Marine National Monuments of the Pacific biology team.[3]

==Transportation==
Access to Jarvis Island is by sea; refuge planning documents state that marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean travel are the only means of reaching the island.[3] The island lies approximately 1,263 nautical miles from U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu, and the voyage typically takes about eight days by ship.[3] Transportation has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[3] Jarvis is commonly visited as part of broader scientific expeditions to central Pacific island refuges, including Baker, Howland, and Johnston Island.[3] Management plans identify the island’s remoteness, lack of affordable and reliable transportation, lack of field infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, and safety and landing constraints as major operational limitations.[3] Supplies for temporary field operations, including fuel for generators, must be transported to the island and handled under spill-prevention, containment, and removal requirements.[1]

==Military==
Jarvis Island’s principal military significance was strategic rather than as a permanent base: the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker so weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] The program also supported U.S. efforts to assert claims to remote central Pacific islands as a check on Japanese territorial expansion.[1] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were primarily young Native Hawaiian men and other male students from Hawaii, and Jarvis, Howland, and Baker together housed more than 130 participants during the 1935–1942 effort.[1][2] Although the island did not become a major military installation, traces of military-era activity remain, including short channels blasted through shallow fringing reefs to facilitate small-boat access and possible hazards left from human habitation during the guano-mining and military periods.[3][4]
